title: Database — Server Health
description: Top-level server-health signals: liveness, saturation, throughput, efficiency, durability subsystem, capacity. Expand the 'How to read this dashboard' row at the top for scope notes and per-panel caveats. Per-query / per-table forensics live in the 'Database — Query & Table Performance' dashboard.

options.content:
### Purpose
Top-level server-health signals: liveness, saturation, throughput, efficiency, durability subsystem, capacity. Per-query / per-table / per-backend-type forensics live in the **Database — Query & Table Performance** dashboard.

### Scope of the `Exporter job` dropdown
Gates the two liveness panels only (**Postgres up** stat, **Scrape liveness history**). Data panels (connections, TPS, cache, WAL, checkpoints, storage) always render against their full upstream series regardless of selection.

### Liveness composite
**Postgres up** is `min(up over selected exporters) × (max(pg_up) or vector(0))` — DOWN if any selected exporter scrape fails OR if postgres-exporter scrapes successfully but reports `pg_up=0`; UNKNOWN if both arms are absent (instrumentation failure, not a DB failure). Vector multiplication rather than `scalar()` so the tile stays stable if a second postgres-exporter target is ever added.

### Scrape liveness history — two failure shapes
- **Step to 0** — the exporter scraped but reported itself DOWN. Real connectivity or auth problem.
- **Gap in the line** — no sample at all. Prometheus wasn't scraping (laptop sleep, docker paused, Prometheus restarted). `connectNulls=false` is intentional so these render as gaps, not interpolated lines.

### Rollback ratio — event-sourcing context
A ROLLBACK here is *not* necessarily an application error. Optimistic-concurrency conflicts on `event_store(aggregate_id, version)` are a designed retry path and under Gatling load can dominate the ratio. Cross-reference with the app-side `wealthpay_account_command_*` timer's `outcome="error"` tag before concluding the DB is reflecting a user-visible incident. Gated to combined rate > 0.5 tps so a handful of rollbacks on an idle DB don't flash red.

### WAL latency averaging caveat
**Fsync latency** and **Write latency** are 5-minute means, so a single short stall (e.g. one 500 ms hiccup among 300 normal fsyncs) is smoothed away. Downstream alerts on these signals must target sustained elevation, not spikes — short-stall detection would require a histogram collector. Pair the two: a write-latency spike without fsync-latency spike points at WAL-buffer contention; both together point at the underlying device.

### Checkpoints: `increase` not `rate`
Checkpoints are discrete, low-cadence events (≈1 per 5 min timed, rare for requested). The honest abstraction is `count per window` — derivative-based rates alias into sawtooth or suppress rare events into invisibility. Any non-zero `requested` value is a tuning signal: WAL filled before the checkpoint timer, so bump `max_wal_size` or shorten `checkpoint_timeout`.

### Checkpoint busy fraction
Sustained >0.95 is tuning pressure, not per-checkpoint duration. 0.9 is the default steady-state for large checkpoints (`checkpoint_completion_target=0.9`). Computed as milliseconds-of-phase ÷ milliseconds-of-5-minute-window, aligned with the adjacent checkpoints panel so the same tick appears on both charts.

### TPS y-axis
Symlog scaling (linear below 1, log above) so a load-test spike at hundreds of tps doesn't visually crush the 0-5 tps baseline. Caveat: on symlog, 0 tps and <0.01 tps look identical — hover for exact values, or cross-check with the block hits/reads panel.

### Buffer cache hit ratio — idle gaps
Idle periods render as gaps, not 100%. A flat-100% line during idle would be indistinguishable from healthy high-traffic. Correlate with the TPS panel to read this one.

### Storage footprint scope
DB size is `datname=wealthpay`; WAL is server-wide. The visual comparison only holds on a single-DB stack. Y-axis is pinned to 0 so a WAL runaway doesn't visually "track" the DB line under auto-scaling.

### Replication slot lag thresholds
Sized against a 1 MB/s baseline WAL rate: warn at 100 MiB (about 100 s of lag), crit at 1 GiB (about 17 min) — both recoverable before disk pressure becomes acute. Filter is `slot_name="debezium.*"` (regex-matched) explicitly so adding a physical replica later doesn't silently change which slot this tile reports on.

### PG17 hazard
Checkpoint columns (`checkpoints_timed_total`, `checkpoints_req_total`, `checkpoint_write_time_total`, `checkpoint_sync_time_total`) relocate from `pg_stat_bgwriter` to the new `pg_stat_checkpointer` view on PG17 and are gated behind postgres-exporter's opt-in `--collector.stat_checkpointer` flag (default off). On upgrade these panels go silently flat until that flag is set. See `docs/postgres-upgrade.md`.

panels.title: Postgres up
panels.description: Composite liveness: DOWN when any selected exporter scrape fails or postgres-exporter reports pg_up=0; UNKNOWN when both arms are absent.

panels.title: Connection utilization
panels.description: Connected backends / max_connections. Sustained >80% is the canonical warning threshold for slot exhaustion — idle sessions still consume a slot.

panels.title: Replication slot lag (Debezium)
panels.description: WAL bytes retained for Debezium. Sustained growth = Debezium isn't keeping up; unreclaimable WAL will fill the disk.

panels.title: Rollback ratio (5m)
panels.description: Rollbacks / (commits + rollbacks) over 5m, gated to >0.5 tps. Optimistic-concurrency retries are a designed path on this stack — cross-check app-side error outcomes before escalating.

panels.title: Transactions per second
panels.description: Commits and rollbacks per second. Flat line when traffic is known to be high = red flag (pool exhausted or scrape broken). Symlog y-axis: 0 and <0.01 tps look identical — hover for exact values.

panels.title: Buffer cache hit ratio
panels.description: blks_hit / (blks_hit + blks_read). Steady-state >0.99 for OLTP; a cliff means the working set exceeded shared_buffers. Idle periods render as gaps — cross-reference with TPS.

panels.title: Block hits / reads
panels.description: Raw rates behind the hit ratio — when the ratio dips, check whether hits dropped or reads spiked (the former is concerning, the latter is usually a large scan).

panels.title: WAL production rate
panels.description: WAL bytes generated per second — the original 'excessive WAL' signal.

panels.title: WAL backpressure (buffers_full rate)
panels.description: Sustained non-zero rate means wal_buffers is undersized — server is stalling on WAL buffer flushes. Tuning signal, not a transient alert.

panels.title: WAL fsync latency (avg ms / sync)
panels.description: Average ms per WAL fsync over 5m. Earliest signal of a WAL device stall — rising = commits queuing behind disk flushes. 5m mean smooths short spikes; alert on sustained elevation. Requires `track_wal_io_timing=on`; otherwise flat.

panels.title: WAL write latency (avg ms / write)
panels.description: Average ms per WAL write over 5m. Pair with fsync latency: write-only spike = WAL-buffer contention; both together = underlying device. Requires `track_wal_io_timing=on`; otherwise flat.

panels.title: Checkpoints per 5 min (timed vs requested)
panels.description: Count of timed vs requested checkpoints per 5m window. Any non-zero `requested` value = WAL filled before the timer; bump max_wal_size or shorten checkpoint_timeout.

panels.title: Checkpoint busy fraction (write + sync phases)
panels.description: Fraction of wall-clock time the checkpointer was in its write or sync phase over 5m. Sustained >0.95 is tuning pressure; 0.9 is the default steady-state for large checkpoints.

panels.title: Storage footprint
panels.description: Database size vs pg_wal directory size. WAL diverging upward from DB size usually means replication lag or archiver failure — cross-reference with the replication slot lag tile.

panels.title: Scrape liveness (history)
panels.description: Per-exporter `up` over the selected time range. Step to 0 = DOWN (connectivity/auth); gap in the line = no sample at all (Prometheus wasn't scraping).
